topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I stared at the screen, the spreadsheet detailing quarterly bonuses, and my stomach tightened into a hard knot. Sarah, the new analyst, had just received her performance review, and the numbers were… staggering. I traced the figures with a trembling finger, the contrast between my modest salary and hers a physical weight pressing down on my chest. Every time she casually mentioned a recent bonus structure, a sharp, unpleasant twist formed in my gut. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and started typing with unnecessary speed, trying to bury the churning feeling beneath productivity.

[story 2]

I watched Mark lean back in his chair, a casual air about him as he discussed his recent compensation package with Brenda. The way his shoulders relaxed, the easy confidence in his voice—it felt like a punch to my own composure. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palm, and took a deliberate, slow sip, letting the bitterness scald my tongue. Internally, a sharp, unwelcome heat rose in my cheeks. I focused intensely on the task at hand, the numbers blurring slightly, while my mind replayed the discrepancy, a bitter taste coating the back of my throat.

[story 3]

It was during the weekly team meeting that the realization hit me, sharp and unwelcome. When David presented the departmental budget projections, the figures for his projected earnings seemed to glow under the harsh conference room lighting. I felt a sudden, intense need to shrink, to become invisible. My hands, resting on the table, started to shake almost imperceptibly. I kept my expression neutral, nodding along to David’s detailed explanation, but inside, a corrosive feeling was bubbling up. I found myself meticulously reviewing my own recent contributions, scrutinizing every hour logged, searching for some hidden flaw, some justification for the gap that now felt so glaringly obvious.
